## Methodological Framework

The goal is not to prove a universal proposition about market efficiency, but
to assess whether a reinforcement learning agent can produce risk-adjusted
performance under realistic and reproducible experimental conditions.

Five principles govern the empirical design throughout. First, data are
processed in chronological order so that the sequential structure of the
problem is preserved and forward-looking information cannot contaminate the
training set. Second, any transformation requiring estimated parameters —
including feature normalization — is fitted on the training split only and
applied without re-fitting to validation and test data. Third, performance
claims are based on held-out test evaluation rather than training behavior.
Fourth, all experimental conditions are specified in external configuration
files so that changes between runs are explicit and auditable. Fifth, the
optimization reward and realized financial return are kept strictly separate:
financial metrics are computed from realized log-return series, not from the
shaped reward signal used during training.

Operational details of the pipeline — data preparation, environment
construction, training loop, and evaluation protocol — are covered in
Chapter 6.
